questions.question: According to the 2025 AHA Guidelines, what is the sequence for a responsive adult with SEVERE choking?
options: B. Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts
questions.rationale: The 2025 Guidelines recommend alternating cycles of 5 back blows (back slaps) and 5 abdominal thrusts for a responsive adult or child with severe obstruction, until relief or unresponsiveness.

questions.question: A choking victim is coughing forcefully and can still speak. What should you do?
options: B. Encourage them to keep coughing and do not interfere
questions.rationale: Forceful coughing and the ability to speak indicate mild obstruction with adequate air exchange. Encourage continued coughing and monitor; intervening can worsen it.

questions.question: How should you relieve severe choking in a pregnant woman?
options: A. Abdominal thrusts over the uterus
B. Chest thrusts (alternating with back blows) instead of abdominal thrusts
questions.rationale: For pregnant or obese patients, chest thrusts replace abdominal thrusts (still alternating with back blows), avoiding pressure on the abdomen/uterus.

questions.question: What is the correct choking sequence for a responsive infant?
options: B. Alternate 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts
C. Abdominal thrusts and back blows
questions.rationale: Infants receive alternating 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts. Abdominal thrusts are not used in infants due to injury risk.

questions.question: Why are abdominal thrusts NOT used in infants?
options: B. Risk of injury to the infant's abdominal organs
questions.rationale: The infant's abdominal organs are vulnerable to injury from abdominal thrusts, so back blows and chest thrusts are used instead.

questions.question: A choking adult becomes unresponsive. What should you do?
options: A. Continue abdominal thrusts on the floor
B. Lower them to the ground, activate the emergency response, and begin CPR
C. Perform a blind finger sweep and wait
questions.rationale: When a choking victim becomes unresponsive, carefully lower them, call for help, and begin CPR starting with compressions; the compressions can help expel the object.

questions.question: During CPR on a victim who was choking, when do you look for the object?
options: B. Each time you open the airway to give breaths — remove it only if visible
questions.rationale: Each time the airway is opened for breaths, look inside and remove an object only if you can clearly see it. Blind finger sweeps are not performed.

questions.question: Why are blind finger sweeps discouraged?
options: B. They can push the object deeper and cause injury
C. They always work and are unnecessary
questions.rationale: Reaching blindly into the mouth can lodge the object deeper, worsening the obstruction, and may injure the victim or rescuer. Only remove a visible object.

questions.question: Where are abdominal thrusts applied on a responsive choking adult?
options: B. Just above the navel, with inward and upward pressure
questions.rationale: Abdominal thrusts are delivered just above the navel and below the ribs, using quick inward and upward pressure to raise intrathoracic pressure and expel the object.

questions.question: What single change did the 2025 Guidelines make to adult/child choking response?
options: B. Added back blows to the sequence (5 back blows + 5 abdominal thrusts)
D. Replaced all thrusts with CPR
questions.rationale: The 2025 update added back blows to the responsive adult/child choking sequence, alternating 5 back blows with 5 abdominal thrusts.

questions.question: Where are back blows delivered?
options: B. Between the shoulder blades with the heel of the hand
questions.rationale: Back blows are delivered between the shoulder blades using the heel of the hand.

questions.question: A large adult is choking and you cannot get your arms around the abdomen for abdominal thrusts. What is the alternative?
options: B. Use chest thrusts (with back blows)
D. Only back blows for 10 minutes
questions.rationale: When abdominal thrusts are not feasible (obese or pregnant patient), chest thrusts on the lower sternum are used, alternating with back blows.

questions.question: How is an infant positioned for back blows?
options: B. Face-down along the forearm with head and neck supported
questions.rationale: For back blows, the infant is held face-down along the rescuer's forearm with the head supported and lower than the trunk; then turned face-up for chest thrusts.

questions.question: How do chest compressions help when a choking victim is unresponsive?
options: B. They can generate pressure that helps expel the foreign object
C. They open the airway permanently
D. They replace the need to look in the mouth
questions.rationale: Chest compressions raise intrathoracic pressure, which can help dislodge and expel the obstructing object, in addition to circulating blood.

questions.question: A responsive choking child (severe obstruction) is standing in front of you. What do you do?
options: B. Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until relief or unresponsiveness
questions.rationale: A responsive child with severe obstruction is managed like an adult: alternating 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object clears or the child becomes unresponsive.

questions.question: Which finding indicates severe (rather than mild) airway obstruction?
options: B. Ability to speak in full sentences
C. Inability to speak, cough, or breathe effectively
questions.rationale: Severe obstruction is marked by the inability to speak, cough effectively, or breathe, often with the universal choking sign. Forceful coughing and speaking indicate mild obstruction.

questions.question: After relieving a choking episode with abdominal thrusts, what is an appropriate follow-up?
options: A. No follow-up is ever needed
B. Advise medical evaluation, as abdominal thrusts can cause internal injury and complications can occur
D. Perform a blind finger sweep to be safe
questions.rationale: Because abdominal thrusts can cause internal injury and aspiration complications can develop, victims should be advised to seek medical evaluation after a significant choking event.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly reflects the 2025 approach across ages for severe, responsive choking?
options: A. Adults: chest thrusts; infants: abdominal thrusts
B. Adults/children: 5 back blows + 5 abdominal thrusts; infants: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts
questions.rationale: The 2025 sequences are: adults and children alternate 5 back blows with 5 abdominal thrusts (chest thrusts if pregnant/obese); infants alternate 5 back blows with 5 chest thrusts (no abdominal thrusts).
